===================================================================
--- /Users/shahe.islam/neovim/lua/plugins/sidekick.lua
+++ /Users/shahe.islam/neovim/lua/plugins/sidekick.lua
@@ -0,0 +1,87 @@
+-- sidekick.nvim - Copilot NES and in-editor AI CLI launcher
+-- OpenCode remains the canonical agent runtime; Sidekick is for learning-oriented suggestions.
+
+local function cli_send(msg)
+  return function()
+    require("sidekick.cli").send({ msg = msg })
+  end
+end
+
+return {
+  {
+    "folke/sidekick.nvim",
+    keys = {
+      {
+        "<leader>asc",
+        function() require("sidekick.cli").toggle({ name = "opencode", focus = true }) end,
+        mode = { "n", "t" },
+        desc = "Toggle Sidekick OpenCode",
+      },
+      {
+        "<leader>ass",
+        function() require("sidekick.cli").select() end,
+        mode = { "n", "t" },
+        desc = "Select Sidekick CLI",
+      },
+      {
+        "<leader>asp",
+        function() require("sidekick.cli").prompt() end,
+        mode = { "n", "x" },
+        desc = "Sidekick Prompt",
+      },
+      {
+        "<leader>ast",
+        cli_send("{this}"),
+        mode = { "n", "x" },
+        desc = "Send This to Sidekick",
+      },
+      {
+        "<leader>asf",
+        cli_send("{file}"),
+        mode = "n",
+        desc = "Send File to Sidekick",
+      },
+      {
+        "<leader>asv",
+        cli_send("{selection}"),
+        mode = "x",
+        desc = "Send Selection to Sidekick",
+      },
+      {
+        "<leader>asj",
+        function() require("sidekick").nes_jump_or_apply() end,
+        mode = { "n", "i" },
+        desc = "Jump/Apply Next Edit Suggestion",
+      },
+      {
+        "<leader>asu",
+        function() require("sidekick.nes").update() end,
+        mode = "n",
+        desc = "Update Next Edit Suggestion",
+      },
+      {
+        "<leader>asx",
+        function() require("sidekick.nes").clear() end,
+        mode = "n",
+        desc = "Clear Next Edit Suggestion",
+      },
+    },
+    opts = {
+      cli = {
+        watch = true,
+        tools = {
+          opencode = {},
+          claude = {},
+          codex = {},
+          gemini = {},
+        },
+        prompts = {
+          explain = "Explain {this} like a mentor. Focus on intent, control flow, and what I should learn.",
+          diagnostics = "Explain these diagnostics and teach me the underlying mistake:\n{diagnostics}",
+          review = "Review {this} for correctness and readability. Do not edit files.",
+          tests = "Suggest tests for {this}. Explain why each test matters before writing code.",
+        },
+      },
+    },
+  },
+}

content.summary:
(b1)
User sought to understand Neovim AI tooling vs OpenCode. Key conclusions established before implementation: OpenCode is already the canonical multi-provider agent runtime; `opencode.nvim` gives OpenCode editor-state awareness with `@this`, `@buffer`, `@buffers`, `@diagnostics`, `@quickfix`, `@diff`, and `@visible`; CodeCompanion’s advantages mostly collapse for this setup because OpenCode supports agents, commands, markdown command files, per-agent model/permissions, and subagent/session workflows. Sidekick remains useful for learning/writing code because its unique value is Copilot-backed Next Edit Suggestions (NES) and a human-in-the-loop AI CLI terminal; Sidekick’s NES requires `copilot-language-server` and Copilot sign-in, but its CLI integration can launch OpenCode/Claude/Codex/Gemini/etc. without Copilot. User specifically requested: “In that case check ~/neovim and remove any AI plugin that isn't opencode.nvim or sidekick.nvim”; then confirmed “Yes add sidekick.nvim now”; then said “Make the changes,” switching from plan to build mode.

Read-only inspection of `~/neovim` found AI plugin specs: keep `lua/plugins/opencode.lua` (`nickjvandyke/opencode.nvim`), remove `lua/plugins/codecompanion.lua` (`olimorris/codecompanion.nvim`), remove `lua/plugins/claude-code.lua` (`greggh/claude-code.nvim`), remove `lua/plugins/agentic.lua` (`carlos-algms/agentic.nvim`), and add Sidekick (not present initially). Related stale references found in `lua/plugins/which-key.lua` groups (`agentic`, `claude-code`, `codecompanion`), `lua/plugins/lualine.lua` disabled filetypes (`AgenticChat`, `AgenticInput`, `AgenticCode`, `AgenticFiles`, `AgenticDiagnostics`), and `lua/plugins/blink-cmp.lua` CodeCompanion completion source/default source. `lazy-lock.json` initially had lock entries for `agentic.nvim`, `claude-code.nvim`, `codecompanion.nvim`, and `opencode.nvim`; no Sidekick entry. `git status --short` in `~/neovim` was clean before edits. `copilot-language-server` was not installed (`command -v copilot-language-server` returned no output). Neovim version is `NVIM v0.12.1`. `lua/plugins/lsp.lua` uses Neovim 0.11+ `vim.lsp.config[...] = server_config` and `vim.lsp.enable(enabled_servers)` with helper `get_lsp_cmd` that skips missing binaries; Copilot LSP still needs to be added using that pattern.

Implemented first atomic edit with `apply_patch`: deleted `~/neovim/lua/plugins/codecompanion.lua`, `~/neovim/lua/plugins/claude-code.lua`, and `~/neovim/lua/plugins/agentic.lua`; added `~/neovim/lua/plugins/sidekick.lua`; updated `~/neovim/lua/plugins/which-key.lua`, `~/neovim/lua/plugins/lualine.lua`, `~/neovim/lua/plugins/blink-cmp.lua`, `~/neovim/CLAUDE.md`, and `~/neovim/AGENTS.md`. New `sidekick.lua` defines `folke/sidekick.nvim` with helper `cli_send(msg)` and keymaps: `<leader>asc` toggles Sidekick OpenCode CLI via `require("sidekick.cli").toggle({ name = "opencode", focus = true })`; `<leader>ass` selects Sidekick CLI; `<leader>asp` opens Sidekick prompt; `<leader>ast` sends `{this}`; `<leader>asf` sends `{file}`; `<leader>asv` sends `{selection}`; `<leader>asj` runs `require("sidekick").nes_jump_or_apply()`; `<leader>asu` runs `require("sidekick.nes").update()`; `<leader>asx` runs `require("sidekick.nes").clear()`. Sidekick opts set `cli.watch = true`, configured CLI tools `opencode`, `claude`, `codex`, `gemini`, and prompt strings for `explain`, `diagnostics`, `review`, and `tests`. `which-key.lua` now removes `<leader>aa`, `<leader>aC`, `<leader>ac` groups and adds `<leader>as` group “sidekick”. `lualine.lua` removed Agentic disabled filetypes, leaving statusline disabled filetypes `{ "dashboard", "alpha", "starter", "snacks_dashboard" }` and empty `winbar = {}`. `blink-cmp.lua` removed `codecompanion` from `sources.default` and deleted the `codecompanion` provider block. `CLAUDE.md` AI Workflow now says to use `opencode.nvim` as the Neovim bridge into OpenCode and `sidekick.nvim` for learning-oriented Copilot NES / CLI assist. `AGENTS.md` workflow contract now says the same. Need continue by adding Copilot LSP config in `lua/plugins/lsp.lua`, cleaning `lazy-lock.json`, installing/updating Sidekick lock if possible, then validating.

17:   if not full_path then

29:   local wasm_source = vim.fs.joinpath(
30:     install_root,

33:     "tree-sitter-twig",
34:     "tree-sitter-twig.wasm"

51:   end
52: 
53:   return extension_path
54: end
55: 
56: -- CRD apiVersion groups that use datreeio/CRDs-catalog for schemas
57: local CRD_GROUPS = {
58:   ["argoproj.io"] = true,           -- Argo CD, Workflows, Rollouts
59:   ["cert-manager.io"] = true,       -- Cert-Manager

62:   ["security.istio.io"] = true,     -- Istio security
63:   ["telemetry.istio.io"] = true,    -- Istio telemetry
64: }
65: 
66: -- Get Kubernetes schema URL based on kind + apiVersion
67: -- Routes to yannh/kubernetes-json-schema for core K8s resources
68: -- Routes to datreeio/CRDs-catalog for CRDs (Argo, Cert-Manager, Prometheus, Istio)
69: local function get_k8s_schema_url(kind, api_version)
70:   local kind_lower = kind:lower()
71:   local group, version = api_version:match("([%w%.%-]+)/(%w+)")
72: 
73:   if not group then
74:     -- Core API (v1) - use yannh
75:     version = api_version

78:       kind_lower, version

87:       group, kind_lower, version
88:     )

92:   -- Remove ".k8s.io" suffix and convert dots to dashes for yannh format
93:   local group_clean = group:gsub("%.k8s%.io$", ""):gsub("%.", "-")
94:   return string.format(
95:     "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/yannh/kubernetes-json-schema/master/v1.31.0-standalone-strict/%s-%s-%s.json",
96:     kind_lower, group_clean, version
97:   )

-- Nix-aware LSP Configuration
-- Prioritizes Nix-provided LSPs (no Mason fallback)
-- This allows per-project LSP versioning via Nix flakes

-- Helper function to get the full path of a command
-- Uses vim.fn.exepath (built-in, no subprocess spawn)
local function get_command_path(cmd)
  local path = vim.fn.exepath(cmd)
  return path ~= "" and path or nil
end

-- Helper function to get command path for LSP
-- Returns { full_path, ...extra_args } or nil if binary not found
-- extra_args should match the upstream nvim-lspconfig cmd (e.g. "--stdio", "start", "serve")
local function get_lsp_cmd(nix_cmd, ...)
  local full_path = get_command_path(nix_cmd)
  if not full_path then
    return nil
  end
  local cmd = { full_path }

state.title: ../neovim/lua/plugins/sidekick.lua

state.output:
# 🤖 `sidekick.nvim` **sidekick.nvim** is your Neovim AI sidekick that integrates Copilot LSP's "Next Edit Suggestions" with a built-in terminal for any AI CLI. Review and apply diffs, chat with AI assistants, and streamline your coding, without leaving your editor. ## ✨ Features - **🤖 Next Edit Suggestions (NES) powered by Copilot LSP** - 🪄 **Automatic Suggestions**: Fetches suggestions automatically when you pause typing or move the cursor. - 🎨 **Rich Diffs**: Visualizes changes with inline and block-level diffs, featuring Treesitter-based syntax highlighting with granular diffing down to the word or character level. - 🧭 **Hunk-by-Hunk Navigation**: Jump through edits to review them one by one before applying. - 📊 **Statusline Integration**: Shows Copilot LSP's status, request progress, and preview text in your statusline. - **💬 Integrated AI CLI Terminal** - 🚀 **Direct Access to AI CLIs**: Interact with your favorite AI command-line tools without leaving Neovim. - 📦 **Pre-configured for Popular Tools**: Out-of-the-box support for Claude, Gemini, Grok, Codex, Copilot CLI, and more. - ✨ **Context-Aware Prompts**: Automatically include file content, cursor position, and diagnostics in your prompts. - 📝 **Prompt Library**: A library of pre-defined prompts for common tasks like explaining code, fixing issues, or writing tests. - 🔄 **Session Persistence**: Keep your CLI sessions alive with `tmux` and `zellij` integration. - 📂 **Automatic File Watching**: Automatically reloads files in Neovim when they are modified by AI tools. - **🔌 Extensible and Customizable** - ⚙️ **Flexible Configuration**: Fine-tune every aspect of the plugin to your liking. - 🧩 **Plugin-Friendly API**: A rich API for integrating with other plugins and building custom workflows. - 🎨 **Customizable UI**: Change the appearance of diffs, signs, and more. ## 📋 Requirements - **Neovim** `>= 0.11.2` or newer - The official [copilot-language-server](https://github.com/github/copilot-language-server-release) LSP server, enabled with `vim.lsp.enable`. Can be installed in multiple ways: 1. install using `npm` or your OS's package manager 2. install with [mason-lspconfig.nvim](https://github.com/mason-org/mason-lspconfig.nvim) 3. [copilot.lua](https://github.com/zbirenbaum/copilot.lua) and [copilot.vim](https://github.com/github/copilot.vim) both bundle the LSP Server in their plugin. - A working `lsp/copilot.lua` configuration. - **TIP:** Included in [nvim-lspconfig](https://github.com/neovim/nvim-lspconfig) - [snacks.nvim](https://github.com/folke/snacks.nvim) for better prompt/tool selection **_(optional)_** - [nvim-treesitter-textobjects](https://github.com/nvim-treesitter/nvim-treesitter-textobjects) **_(`main` branch)_** for `{function}` and `{class}` context variables **_(optional)_** - AI cli tools, such as Codex, Claude, Copilot, Gemini, … **_(optional)_** see the [🤖 AI CLI Integration](#-ai-cli-integration) section for details. - [lsof](https://man7.org/linux/man-pages/man8/lsof.8.html) and [ps](https://man7.org/linux/man-pages/man1/ps.1.html) are used on Unix-like systems to detect running AI CLI tool sessions. **_(optional, but recommended)_** ## 🚀 Quick Start 1. **Install** the plugin with your package manager (see below) 2. **Configure Copilot LSP** - must be enabled with `vim.lsp.enable` 3. **Check health**: `:checkhealth sidekick` 4. **Sign in to Copilot**: `:LspCopilotSignIn` 5. **Try it out**: - Type some code and pause - watch for Next Edit Suggestions appearing - Press `` to navigate through or apply suggestions - Use `aa` to open AI CLI tools > [!NOTE] > **New to Next Edit Suggestions?** Unlike inline completions, NES suggests entire refactorings or multi-line changes anywhere in your file - think of it as Copilot's "big picture" suggestions. ## 📦 Installation Install with your favorite manager. With [lazy.nvim](https://github.com/folke/lazy.nvim): ```lua { "folke/sidekick.nvim", opts = { -- add any options here cli = { mux = { backend = "zellij", enabled = true, }, }, }, keys = { { "", function() -- if there is a next edit, jump to it, otherwise apply it if any if not require("sidekick").nes_jump_or_apply() then return "" -- fallback to normal tab end end, expr = true, desc = "Goto/Apply Next Edit Suggestion", }, { "", function() require("sidekick.cli").focus() end, desc = "Sidekick Focus", mode = { "n", "t", "i", "x" }, }, { "aa", function() require("sidekick.cli").toggle() end, desc = "Sidekick Toggle CLI", }, { "as", function() require("sidekick.cli").select() end, -- Or to select only installed tools: -- require("sidekick.cli").select({ filter = { installed = true } }) desc = "Select CLI", }, { "ad", function() require("sidekick.cli").close() end, desc = "Detach a CLI Session", }, { "at", function() require("sidekick.cli").send({ msg = "{this}" }) end, mode = { "x", "n" }, desc = "Send This", }, { "af", function() require("sidekick.cli").send({ msg = "{file}" }) end, desc = "Send File", }, { "av", function() require("sidekick.cli").send({ msg = "{selection}" }) end, mode = { "x" }, desc = "Send Visual Selection", }, { "ap", function() require("sidekick.cli").prompt() end, mode = { "n", "x" }, desc = "Sidekick Select Prompt", }, -- Example of a keybinding to open Claude directly { "ac", function() require("sidekick.cli").toggle({ name = "claude", focus = true }) end, desc = "Sidekick Toggle Claude", }, }, } ``` > [!TIP] > It's a good idea to run `:checkhealth sidekick` after install.
Integrate <Tab> in insert mode with blink.cmp ```lua { "saghen/blink.cmp", ---@module 'blink.cmp' ---@type blink.cmp.Config opts = { keymap = { [""] = { "snippet_forward", function() -- sidekick next edit suggestion return require("sidekick").nes_jump_or_apply() end, function() -- if you are using Neovim's native inline completions return vim.lsp.inline_completion.get() end, "fallback", }, }, }, } ```
Custom <Tab> integration for insert mode ```lua { "folke/sidekick.nvim", opts = { -- add any options here }, keys = { { "", function() -- if there is a next edit, jump to it, otherwise apply it if any if require("sidekick").nes_jump_or_apply() then return -- jumped or applied end -- if you are using Neovim's native inline completions if vim.lsp.inline_completion.get() then return end -- any other things (like snippets) you want to do on go here. -- fall back to normal tab return "" end, mode = { "i", "n" }, expr = true, desc = "Goto/Apply Next Edit Suggestion", }, }, } ```
After installation sign in with `:LspCopilotSignIn` if prompted. ## ⚙️ Configuration The module ships with safe defaults and exposes everything through `require("sidekick").setup({ ... })`.
Default settings ```lua ---@class sidekick.Config local defaults = { nes = { ---@type boolean|fun(buf:integer):boolean? enabled = function(buf) return vim.g.sidekick_nes ~= false and vim.b.sidekick_nes ~= false end, debounce = 100, trigger = { -- events that trigger sidekick next edit suggestions events = { "ModeChanged i:n", "TextChanged", "User SidekickNesDone" }, }, clear = { -- events that clear the current next edit suggestion events = { "TextChangedI", "InsertEnter" }, esc = true, -- clear next edit suggestions when pressing }, ---@class sidekick.diff.Opts ---@field inline? "words"|"chars"|false Enable inline diffs ---@field show? "always"|"cursor" `cursor` will only show the diff when the cursor is at the edit position. diff = { inline = "words", show = "always", }, signs = true, -- show signs for next edit suggestions jumplist = true, -- add an entry to the jumplist }, -- Work with AI cli tools directly from within Neovim cli = { watch = true, -- notify Neovim of file changes done by AI CLI tools ---@class sidekick.win.Opts win = { --- This is run when a new terminal is created, before starting it. --- Here you can change window options `terminal.opts`. ---@param terminal sidekick.cli.Terminal config = function(terminal) end, wo = {}, ---@type vim.wo bo = {}, ---@type vim.bo layout = "right", ---@type "float"|"left"|"bottom"|"top"|"right" --- Options used when layout is "float" ---@type vim.api.keyset.win_config float = { width = 0.9, height = 0.9, }, -- Options used when layout is "left"|"bottom"|"top"|"right" ---@type vim.api.keyset.win_config split = { width = 80, -- set to 0 for default split width height = 20, -- set to 0 for default split height }, --- CLI Tool Keymaps (default mode is `t`) ---@type table keys = { buffers = { "", "buffers" , mode = "nt", desc = "open buffer picker" }, files = { "", "files" , mode = "nt", desc = "open file picker" }, hide_n = { "q" , "hide" , mode = "n" , desc = "hide the terminal window" }, hide_ctrl_q = { "", "hide" , mode = "n" , desc = "hide the terminal window" }, hide_ctrl_dot = { "", "hide" , mode = "nt", desc = "hide the terminal window" }, hide_ctrl_z = { "", "blur" , mode = "nt", desc = "go back to the previous window without hiding the terminal" }, prompt = { "", "prompt" , mode = "t" , desc = "insert prompt or context" }, stopinsert = { "", "stopinsert", mode = "t" , desc = "enter normal mode" }, -- Navigate windows in terminal mode. Only active when: -- * layout is not "float" -- * there is another window in the direction -- With the default layout of "right", only `` will be mapped nav_left = { "", "nav_left" , expr = true, desc = "navigate to the left window" }, nav_down = { "", "nav_down" , expr = true, desc = "navigate to the below window" }, nav_up = { "", "nav_up" , expr = true, desc = "navigate to the above window" }, nav_right = { "", "nav_right" , expr = true, desc = "navigate to the right window" }, }, ---@type fun(dir:"h"|"j"|"k"|"l")? --- Function that handles navigation between windows. --- Defaults to `vim.cmd.wincmd`. Used by the `nav_*` keymaps. nav = nil, }, ---@class sidekick.cli.Mux ---@field backend? "tmux"|"zellij" Multiplexer backend to persist CLI sessions mux = { backend = vim.env.ZELLIJ and "zellij" or "tmux", -- default to tmux unless zellij is detected enabled = false, -- terminal: new sessions will be created for each CLI tool and shown in a Neovim terminal -- window: when run inside a terminal multiplexer, new sessions will be created in a new tab -- split: when run inside a terminal multiplexer, new sessions will be created in a new split -- NOTE: zellij only supports `terminal` create = "terminal", ---@type "terminal"|"window"|"split" split = { vertical = true, -- vertical or horizontal split size = 0.5, -- size of the split (0-1 for percentage) }, }, --- Actual cli tool config is loaded from the runtime path `sk/cli/{tool}.lua` and merged with the config below. --- For default configs, see https://github.com/folke/sidekick.nvim/tree/main/sk/cli ---@type table tools = { aider = {}, amazon_q = {}, claude = {}, codex = {}, copilot = {}, crush = {}, cursor = {}, gemini = {}, grok = {}, opencode = {}, pi = {}, qwen = {}, }, --- Add custom context. See `lua/sidekick/context/init.lua` ---@type table context = {}, ---@type table prompts = { changes = "Can you review my changes?", diagnostics = "Can you help me fix the diagnostics in {file}?\n{diagnostics}", diagnostics_all = "Can you help me fix these diagnostics?\n{diagnostics_all}", document = "Add documentation to {function|line}", explain = "Explain {this}", fix = "Can you fix {this}?", optimize = "How can {this} be optimized?", review = "Can you review {file} for any issues or improvements?", tests = "Can you write tests for {this}?", -- simple context prompts buffers = "{buffers}", file = "{file}", line = "{line}", position = "{position}", quickfix = "{quickfix}", selection = "{selection}", ["function"] = "{function}", class = "{class}", }, -- preferred picker for selecting files ---@alias sidekick.picker "snacks"|"telescope"|"fzf-lua" picker = "snacks", ---@type sidekick.picker }, copilot = { -- track copilot's status with `didChangeStatus` status = { enabled = true, level = vim.log.levels.WARN, -- set to vim.log.levels.OFF to disable notifications -- level = vim.log.levels.OFF, }, }, ui = { icons = { nes = " ", attached = " ", started = " ", installed = " ", missing = " ", external_attached = "󰖩 ", external_started = "󰖪 ", terminal_attached = " ", terminal_started = " ", }, }, debug = false, -- enable debug logging } ```
## ✏️ Next Edit Suggestions (NES) Copilot NES requests run automatically when you leave insert mode, modify text in normal mode, or after applying an edit.
CmdLua
:Sidekick nes apply Apply active text edits ```lua ---@return boolean applied require("sidekick.nes").apply() ```
:Sidekick nes clear Clear all active edits ```lua require("sidekick.nes").clear() ```
:Sidekick nes disable ```lua require("sidekick.nes").disable() ```
:Sidekick nes enable ```lua ---@param enable? boolean require("sidekick.nes").enable(enable) ```
Check if any edits are active in the current buffer ```lua require("sidekick.nes").have() ```
:Sidekick nes jump Jump to the start of the active edit ```lua ---@return boolean jumped require("sidekick.nes").jump() ```
:Sidekick nes toggle ```lua require("sidekick.nes").toggle() ```
:Sidekick nes update Request new edits from the LSP server (if any) ```lua require("sidekick.nes").update() ```
## 🤖 AI CLI Integration Sidekick ships with a lightweight terminal wrapper so you can talk to local AI CLI tools without leaving Neovim. Each tool runs in its own scratch terminal window and shares helper prompts that bundle buffer context, the current cursor position, and diagnostics when requested.
CmdLua
:Sidekick cli close ```lua ---@param opts? sidekick.cli.Hide ---@overload fun(name: string) require("sidekick.cli").close(opts) ```
:Sidekick cli focus Toggle focus of the terminal window if it is already open ```lua ---@param opts? sidekick.cli.Show ---@overload fun(name: string) require("sidekick.cli").focus(opts) ```
:Sidekick cli hide ```lua ---@param opts? sidekick.cli.Hide ---@overload fun(name: string) require("sidekick.cli").hide(opts) ```
:Sidekick cli prompt Select a prompt to send ```lua ---@param opts? sidekick.cli.Prompt|{cb:nil} ---@overload fun(cb:fun(msg?:string)) require("sidekick.cli").prompt(opts) ```
Render a message template or prompt ```lua ---@param opts? sidekick.cli.Message|string require("sidekick.cli").render(opts) ```
:Sidekick cli select Start or attach to a CLI tool ```lua ---@param opts? sidekick.cli.Select|{cb:nil}|{focus?:boolean} ---@overload fun(cb:fun(state?:sidekick.cli.State)) require("sidekick.cli").select(opts) ```
:Sidekick cli send Send a message or prompt to a CLI ```lua ---@param opts? sidekick.cli.Send ---@overload fun(msg:string) require("sidekick.cli").send(opts) ```
:Sidekick cli show ```lua ---@param opts? sidekick.cli.Show ---@overload fun(name: string) require("sidekick.cli").show(opts) ```
:Sidekick cli toggle ```lua ---@param opts? sidekick.cli.Show ---@overload fun(name: string) require("sidekick.cli").toggle(opts) ```
### Prompts & Context Sidekick comes with a set of predefined prompts that you can use with your AI tools. You can also use context variables in your prompts to include information about the current file, selection, diagnostics, and more.
Available Prompts - **changes**: `Can you review my changes?` - **diagnostics**: `Can you help me fix the diagnostics in {file}?\n{diagnostics}` - **diagnostics_all**: `Can you help me fix these diagnostics?\n{diagnostics_all}` - **document**: `Add documentation to {position}` - **explain**: `Explain {this}` - **fix**: `Can you fix {this}?` - **optimize**: `How can {this} be optimized?` - **review**: `Can you review {file} for any issues or improvements?` - **tests**: `Can you write tests for {this}?` - **quickfix**: `{quickfix}` (current quickfix entries).
Available Context Variables - `{buffers}`: A list of all open buffers. - `{file}`: The current file path. - `{position}`: The cursor position in the current file. - `{line}`: The current line. - `{selection}`: The visual selection. - `{diagnostics}`: The diagnostics for the current buffer. - `{diagnostics_all}`: All diagnostics in the workspace. - `{quickfix}`: The current quickfix list, including title and formatted items. - `{function}`: The function at cursor (Tree-sitter) - returns location like `function foo @file:10:5`. - `{class}`: The class/struct at cursor (Tree-sitter) - returns location. - `{this}`: A special context variable. If the current buffer is a file, it resolves to `{position}`. Otherwise, it resolves to the literal string "this" and appends the current `{selection}` to the prompt.
### Snacks.nvim Picker Integration If you're using [snacks.nvim](https://github.com/folke/snacks.nvim), you can send picker selections directly to Sidekick's AI CLI tools. This is useful for sending search results, grep matches, or file selections as context.
Example Snacks picker configuration ```lua { "folke/snacks.nvim", optional = true, opts = { picker = { actions = { sidekick_send = function(...) return require("sidekick.cli.picker.snacks").send(...) end, }, win = { input = { keys = { [""] = { "sidekick_send", mode = { "n", "i" }, }, }, }, }, }, }, } ``` With this configuration, pressing `` in any Snacks picker will send the selected items to your current AI CLI session. The integration automatically handles: - File selections with full paths - Grep results with line numbers and positions - Multiple selections (sends all selected items) - Position ranges for precise context
### CLI Keymaps You can customize the keymaps for the CLI window by setting the `cli.win.keys` option. The default keymaps are: - `q` (in normal mode): Hide the terminal window. - `` (in terminal mode): Hide the terminal window. - ``: Leave the CLI window. - ``: Insert prompt or context.
Example of how to override the default keymaps ```lua { "folke/sidekick.nvim", opts = { cli = { win = { keys = { -- override the default hide keymap hide_n = { "q", "hide", mode = "n" }, -- add a new keymap to say hi say_hi = { "", function(t) t:send("hi!") end, }, }, }, }, }, } ```
### Default CLI tools Sidekick preconfigures popular AI CLIs. Run `:checkhealth sidekick` to see which ones are installed. | Tool | Description | Installation | | ----------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | | [`aider`](https://github.com/Aider-AI/aider) | AI pair programmer | `pip install aider-chat` or `pipx install aider-chat` | | [`amazon_q`](https://github.com/aws/amazon-q-developer-cli) | Amazon Q Developer | [Install guide](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/amazonq/latest/qdeveloper-ug/command-line-getting-started-installing.html) | | [`claude`](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code) | Claude Code CLI | [See Claude Code docs](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/overview#get-started) | [`codex`](https://github.com/openai/codex) | OpenAI Codex CLI | See [OpenAI docs](https://github.com/openai/codex) | | [`copilot`](https://github.com/github/copilot-cli) | GitHub Copilot CLI | `npm install -g @githubnext/github-copilot-cli` | | [`crush`](https://github.com/charmbracelet/crush) | Charm's AI assistant | See [installation](https://github.com/charmbracelet/crush) | | [`cursor`](https://cursor.com/cli) | Cursor CLI agent | See [Cursor docs](https://cursor.com/cli) | | [`gemini`](https://github.com/google-gemini/gemini-cli) | Google Gemini CLI | See [repo](https://github.com/google-gemini/gemini-cli) | | [`grok`](https://github.com/superagent-ai/grok-cli) | xAI Grok CLI | See [repo](https://github.com/superagent-ai/grok-cli) | | [`opencode`](https://github.com/sst/opencode) | OpenCode CLI | `npm install -g opencode` | | [`qwen`](https://github.com/QwenLM/qwen-code) | Alibaba Qwen Code | See [repo](https://github.com/QwenLM/qwen-code) | > [!TIP] > After installing tools, restart Neovim or run `:Sidekick cli select` to see them available. ## 🚀 Commands Sidekick provides a `:Sidekick` command that allows you to interact with the plugin from the command line. The command is a thin wrapper around the Lua API, so you can use it to do anything that the Lua API can do. ### Command Structure The command structure is simple: ``` :Sidekick [args] ``` - ``: The name of the module you want to use (e.g., `nes`, `cli`). - ``: The name of the command you want to execute. - `[args]`: Optional arguments for the command. The arguments are parsed as a Lua table. For example, to show the CLI window for the `claude` tool, you can use the following command: ``` :Sidekick cli show name=claude ``` This is equivalent to the following Lua code: ```lua require("sidekick.cli").show({ name = "claude" }) ```
Available Commands Here's a list of the available commands: **NES (`nes`)** - `enable`: Enable Next Edit Suggestions. - `disable`: Disable Next Edit Suggestions. - `toggle`: Toggle Next Edit Suggestions. - `update`: Trigger a new suggestion. - `clear`: Clear the current suggestion. **CLI (`cli`)** - `show`: Show the CLI window. - `toggle`: Toggle the CLI window. - `hide`: Hide the CLI window. - `close`: Close the CLI window. - `focus`: Focus the CLI window. - `select`: Select a CLI tool to open. - `send`: Send a message to the current CLI tool. - `prompt`: Select a prompt to send to the current CLI tool.
Examples Here are some examples of how to use the `:Sidekick` command: - Toggle the CLI window: ``` :Sidekick cli toggle ``` Lua equivalent: ```lua require("sidekick.cli").toggle() ``` - Send the visual selection to the current CLI tool: ``` :'<,'>Sidekick cli send msg="{selection}" ``` Lua equivalent: ```lua require("sidekick.cli").send({ msg = "{selection}" }) ``` - Show the CLI window for the `grok` tool and focus it: ``` :Sidekick cli show name=grok focus=true ``` Lua equivalent: ```lua require("sidekick.cli").show({ name = "grok", focus = true }) ```
## 📟 Statusline Integration Using the `require("sidekick.status")` API, you can easily integrate **Copilot LSP** and **CLI sessions** in your statusline.
Example for lualine.nvim ```lua { "nvim-lualine/lualine.nvim", opts = function(_, opts) opts.sections = opts.sections or {} opts.sections.lualine_c = opts.sections.lualine_c or {} -- Copilot status table.insert(opts.sections.lualine_c, { function() return " " end, color = function() local status = require("sidekick.status").get() if status then return status.kind == "Error" and "DiagnosticError" or status.busy and "DiagnosticWarn" or "Special" end end, cond = function() local status = require("sidekick.status") return status.get() ~= nil end, }) -- CLI session status table.insert(opts.sections.lualine_x, 2, { function() local status = require("sidekick.status").cli() return " " .. (#status > 1 and #status or "") end, cond = function() return #require("sidekick.status").cli() > 0 end, color = function() return "Special" end, }) end, } ```
## ❓ FAQ ### Does sidekick.nvim replace Copilot's inline suggestions? No! NES complements inline suggestions. They serve different purposes: - **Inline completions**: Quick, as-you-type suggestions (use copilot.lua or native `vim.lsp.inline_completion`) - **NES**: Larger refactorings and multi-line changes after you pause You'll want both for the best experience. ### How is this different from copilot.lua or copilot.vim? `copilot.lua` and `copilot.vim` provide **inline completions** (suggestions as you type). `sidekick.nvim` adds: - **Next Edit Suggestions (NES)**: Multi-line refactorings and context-aware edits across your file - **AI CLI Integration**: Built-in terminal for Claude, Gemini, and other AI tools Use them together for the complete experience! ### NES not showing suggestions? 1. Run `:checkhealth sidekick` to verify your setup 2. Check Copilot is signed in: `:LspCopilotSignIn` 3. Verify the LSP is attached: `:lua vim.print(require("sidekick.config").get_client())` 4. Try manually triggering: `:Sidekick nes update` ### CLI tools not starting? 1. Verify the tool is installed: `which claude` (or your tool name) 2. Check `:checkhealth sidekick` for tool installation status 3. Try running the tool directly in your terminal first 4. Check for errors with `:messages` after attempting to start ### Terminal sessions not persisting? Make sure you have tmux or zellij installed and enable the multiplexer: ```lua opts = { cli = { mux = { enabled = true, backend = "tmux", -- or "zellij" }, }, } ``` ### Do I need a GitHub Copilot subscription? Yes, but only for the **NES feature** (Next Edit Suggestions). The **AI CLI integration** works independently with any CLI tool (Claude, Gemini, etc.) and doesn't require Copilot. ### Can I use this without NES, just for CLI tools? Absolutely! Just disable NES: ```lua opts = { nes = { enabled = false }, } ``` ### Will this work with Neovim 0.10? No, Neovim **>= 0.11.2** is required for the LSP features and API used by sidekick.nvim. ### How do I add my own AI tool? Add it to the `cli.tools` configuration: ```lua opts = { cli = { tools = { my_tool = { cmd = { "my-ai-cli", "--flag" }, -- Optional: custom keymaps for this tool keys = { submit = { "", function(t) t:send("\n") end }, }, }, }, }, } ``` ### How do I create custom prompts? Add them to your config: ```lua opts = { cli = { prompts = { refactor = "Please refactor {this} to be more maintainable", security = "Review {file} for security vulnerabilities", custom = function(ctx) return "Current file: " .. ctx.buf .. " at line " .. ctx.row end, }, }, } ``` Then use with `ap` or `:Sidekick cli prompt`.

---@param bufnr integer,
---@param client vim.lsp.Client
local function sign_in(bufnr, client)
  client:request(
    ---@diagnostic disable-next-line: param-type-mismatch
    'signIn',
    vim.empty_dict(),
    function(err, result)
      if err then
        vim.notify(err.message, vim.log.levels.ERROR)
        return
      end
      if result.command then
        local code = result.userCode
        local command = result.command
        vim.fn.setreg('+', code)
        vim.fn.setreg('*', code)
        local continue = vim.fn.confirm(
          'Copied your one-time code to clipboard.\n' .. 'Open the browser to complete the sign-in process?',
          '&Yes\n&No'
        )
        if continue == 1 then
          client:exec_cmd(command, { bufnr = bufnr }, function(cmd_err, cmd_result)
            if cmd_err then
              vim.notify(cmd_err.message, vim.log.levels.ERROR)
              return
            end
            if cmd_result.status == 'OK' then
              vim.notify('Signed in as ' .. cmd_result.user .. '.')
            end
          end)
        end
      end

      if result.status == 'PromptUserDeviceFlow' then
        vim.notify('Enter your one-time code ' .. result.userCode .. ' in ' .. result.verificationUri)
      elseif result.status == 'AlreadySignedIn' then
        vim.notify('Already signed in as ' .. result.user .. '.')
      end
    end
  )
end

---@param client vim.lsp.Client
local function sign_out(_, client)
  client:request(
    ---@diagnostic disable-next-line: param-type-mismatch
    'signOut',
    vim.empty_dict(),
    function(err, result)
      if err then
        vim.notify(err.message, vim.log.levels.ERROR)
        return
      end
      if result.status == 'NotSignedIn' then
        vim.notify('Not signed in.')
      end
    end
  )
end

---@type vim.lsp.Config
return {
  cmd = {
    'copilot-language-server',
    '--stdio',
  },
  root_markers = { '.git' },
  init_options = {
    editorInfo = {
      name = 'Neovim',
      version = tostring(vim.version()),
    },
    editorPluginInfo = {
      name = 'Neovim',
      version = tostring(vim.version()),
    },
  },
  settings = {
    telemetry = {
      telemetryLevel = 'all',
    },
  },
  on_attach = function(client, bufnr)
    vim.api.nvim_buf_create_user_command(bufnr, 'LspCopilotSignIn', function()
      sign_in(bufnr, client)
    end, { desc = 'Sign in Copilot with GitHub' })
    vim.api.nvim_buf_create_user_command(bufnr, 'LspCopilotSignOut', function()
      sign_out(bufnr, client)
    end, { desc = 'Sign out Copilot with GitHub' })
  end,
}

+  client:request("signIn", vim.empty_dict(), function(err, result)

+      vim.notify(err.message, vim.log.levels.ERROR)
+      return

+        "&Yes\n&No"

+        client:exec_cmd(result.command, { bufnr = bufnr }, function(cmd_err, cmd_result)
+          if cmd_err then
+            vim.notify(cmd_err.message, vim.log.levels.ERROR)

+
+          if cmd_result.status == "OK" then
+            vim.notify("Signed in as " .. cmd_result.user .. ".")

**Inspecting code components**

I need to look into the lazy-lock and perform a git diff. Searching for stale elements is a good next step. Using Grep within Neovim will help me with code companion tasks. I should also take some time to read the lock file thoroughly. It seems like using a multi-tool approach will be beneficial for this task. I want to ensure everything is in order and efficiently addressed! Let's get to it!

1: -- Hot-reload configuration for Claude Code integration
2: -- Automatically reloads buffers when files change externally
3: -- Based on: https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/claude-code-neovim
4: 
5: local M = {}
6: 
7: -- State for file watching
8: local watchers = {}
9: local debounce_timer = nil
10: local DEBOUNCE_MS = 100
11: 
12: -- Helper: Check if buffer should be skipped
13: local function should_skip_buffer(bufnr)
14:   -- Skip if buffer is modified (don't lose local changes)
15:   if vim.bo[bufnr].modified then
16:     return true
17:   end
18: 
19:   -- Skip special buffer types
20:   local buftype = vim.bo[bufnr].buftype
21:   if buftype ~= "" then
22:     return true
23:   end
24: 
25:   -- Skip special URI schemes (diffview://, oil://, etc.)
26:   local bufname = vim.api.nvim_buf_get_name(bufnr)
27:   if bufname:match("^%w+://") then
28:     return true
29:   end
30: 
31:   return false
32: end
33: 
34: -- Reload visible buffers in current tab
35: local function reload_visible_buffers()
36:   -- Get all windows in current tab
37:   local wins = vim.api.nvim_tabpage_list_wins(0)
38: 
39:   for _, win in ipairs(wins) do
40:     local bufnr = vim.api.nvim_win_get_buf(win)
41:     if not should_skip_buffer(bufnr) then
42:       -- checktime triggers autoread for this buffer

1: -- Hot-reload configuration for Claude Code integration
2: -- Automatically reloads buffers when files change externally
3: -- Based on: https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/claude-code-neovim
4: 
5: local M = {}
6: 
7: -- State for file watching
8: local watchers = {}
9: local debounce_timer = nil
10: local DEBOUNCE_MS = 100
11: 
12: -- Helper: Check if buffer should be skipped
13: local function should_skip_buffer(bufnr)
14:   -- Skip if buffer is modified (don't lose local changes)
15:   if vim.bo[bufnr].modified then
16:     return true
17:   end
18: 
19:   -- Skip special buffer types
20:   local buftype = vim.bo[bufnr].buftype
21:   if buftype ~= "" then
22:     return true
23:   end
24: 
25:   -- Skip special URI schemes (diffview://, oil://, etc.)
26:   local bufname = vim.api.nvim_buf_get_name(bufnr)
27:   if bufname:match("^%w+://") then
28:     return true
29:   end
30: 
31:   return false
32: end
33: 
34: -- Reload visible buffers in current tab
35: local function reload_visible_buffers()
36:   -- Get all windows in current tab
37:   local wins = vim.api.nvim_tabpage_list_wins(0)
38: 
39:   for _, win in ipairs(wins) do
40:     local bufnr = vim.api.nvim_win_get_buf(win)
41:     if not should_skip_buffer(bufnr) then
42:       -- checktime triggers autoread for this buffer
43:       vim.api.nvim_buf_call(bufnr, function()
44:         vim.cmd("silent! checktime")
45:       end)
46:     end
47:   end...

# Skill: neovim

# Neovim Config — Modify, Add, Debug

Guided workflow for safely modifying this neovim configuration. Routes to the correct file, follows conventions, and validates changes.

## Arguments

- `$ARGUMENTS` — Natural language description of what to do. Examples:
  - `add plugin neo-tree` → Phase 1 (Add Plugin)
  - `add keymap for format on save` → Phase 2 (Add Keymap)
  - `configure gopls` → Phase 3 (LSP)
  - `fix colorscheme bleeding` → Phase 4 (Debug)
  - No args → show this help and ask what to do

## Phase 1: Add Plugin

### Step 1 — Check for conflicts

```bash
# Check if plugin or similar functionality already exists
grep -r "PLUGIN_NAME_OR_KEYWORD" lua/plugins/ --include="*.lua" -l
# Check lazy-lock for existing installation
grep "PLUGIN_NAME" lazy-lock.json
```

If the plugin or equivalent functionality already exists, report it and ask whether to replace or extend.

1: ---
2: ticket: "TASK"
3: title: "Fix client.request deprecation warnings in Neovim 0.12"
4: created: "2026-04-18T21:42:00Z"
5: last_updated: "2026-04-18T20:56:24Z"
6: ---
7: 
8: # Plan: TASK — client.request deprecation fix
9: 
10: ## Objective
11: 
12: Eliminate the `client.request is deprecated` notification that appears in Neovim 0.12.1 when plugins use dot-notation LSP client methods (`client.request()` instead of `client:request()`).
13: 
14: ## Approach
15: 
16: 1. Identify source of deprecation warnings
17: 2. Filter warnings from plugins in the vim.notify override (since noice routing is bypassed)
18: 3. Update lazy-lock.json with latest plugin versions (some may have fixes)
19: 
20: ## Progress
21: 
22: - [x] Root cause investigation
23: - [x] Add deprecation filter in vim.notify override (noice.lua)
24: - [x] Update lazy-lock.json with latest plugin commits
25: 
26: ## Key Decisions
27: 
28: - User's own config is clean — ALL deprecation comes from third-party plugins (blink.cmp, nvim-lspconfig, rustaceanvim, fzf-lua, nvim-ufo, oil.nvim, yazi.nvim, nvim-nio, mini.nvim)
29: - The vim.notify override in noice.lua (line 131) sends notifications directly to nvim-notify, bypassing noice's route filters — this is why the existing `{ find = "checkhealth vim%.deprecated" }` filter on line 362 never fires
30: - Fix goes in the vim.notify override itself (not noice routes) since noice.notify is disabled (`enabled = false`)
31:

+name: CI
+
+on:
+  push:
+  pull_request:
+
+jobs:
+  validate:
+    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
+
+    steps:
+      - name: Checkout repository
+        uses: actions/checkout@v4
+
+      - name: Install Neovim
+        run: |
+          curl -fsSL -o nvim.tar.gz https://github.com/neovim/neovim/releases/download/stable/nvim-linux-x86_64.tar.gz
+          tar -xzf nvim.tar.gz
+          echo "$PWD/nvim-linux-x86_64/bin" >> "$GITHUB_PATH"
+
+      - name: Install config into NVIM_APPNAME
+        run: |
+          mkdir -p "$HOME/.config/nvim-mini"
+          cp -R . "$HOME/.config/nvim-mini"
+
+      - name: Sync plugins
+        env:
+          NVIM_APPNAME: nvim-mini
+        run: nvim --headless "+Lazy! sync" +qa
+
+      - name: Headless startup check
+        env:
+          NVIM_APPNAME: nvim-mini
+        run: nvim --headless +qa
+
+      - name: Custom health check
+        env:
+          NVIM_APPNAME: nvim-mini
+        run: nvim --headless "+checkhealth nvim_mini" +qa
+
+      - name: Parley review test
+        run: nvim --headless -l tests/parley_review_spec.lua

1: ---
2: ticket: "TASK"
3: title: "Fix client.request deprecation warnings in Neovim 0.12"
4: created: "2026-04-18T21:42:00Z"
5: last_updated: "2026-04-18T20:56:24Z"
6: ---
7: 
8: # Plan: TASK — client.request deprecation fix
9: 
10: ## Objective
11: 
12: Eliminate the `client.request is deprecated` notification that appears in Neovim 0.12.1 when plugins use dot-notation LSP client methods (`client.request()` instead of `client:request()`).
13: 
14: ## Approach
15: 
16: 1. Identify source of deprecation warnings
17: 2. Filter warnings from plugins in the vim.notify override (since noice routing is bypassed)
18: 3. Update lazy-lock.json with latest plugin versions (some may have fixes)
19: 
20: ## Progress
21: 
22: - [x] Root cause investigation
23: - [x] Add deprecation filter in vim.notify override (noice.lua)
24: - [x] Update lazy-lock.json with latest plugin commits
25: 
26: ## Key Decisions
27: 
28: - User's own config is clean — ALL deprecation comes from third-party plugins (blink.cmp, nvim-lspconfig, rustaceanvim, fzf-lua, nvim-ufo, oil.nvim, yazi.nvim, nvim-nio, mini.nvim)
29: - The vim.notify override in noice.lua (line 131) sends notifications directly to nvim-notify, bypassing noice's route filters — this is why the existing `{ find = "checkhealth vim%.deprecated" }` filter on line 362 never fires
30: - Fix goes in the vim.notify override itself (not noice routes) since noice.notify is disabled (`enabled = false`)
31:

--- .github/workflows/ci.yml	
+++ .github/workflows/ci.yml	
@@ -0,0 +1,42 @@
+name: CI
+
+on:
+  push:
+  pull_request:
+
+jobs:
+  validate:
+    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
+
+    steps:
+      - name: Checkout repository
+        uses: actions/checkout@v4
+
+      - name: Install Neovim
+        run: |
+          curl -fsSL -o nvim.tar.gz https://github.com/neovim/neovim/releases/download/stable/nvim-linux-x86_64.tar.gz
+          tar -xzf nvim.tar.gz
+          echo "$PWD/nvim-linux-x86_64/bin" >> "$GITHUB_PATH"
+
+      - name: Install config into NVIM_APPNAME
+        run: |
+          mkdir -p "$HOME/.config/nvim-mini"
+          cp -R . "$HOME/.config/nvim-mini"
+
+      - name: Sync plugins
+        env:
+          NVIM_APPNAME: nvim-mini
+        run: nvim --headless "+Lazy! sync" +qa
+
+      - name: Headless startup check
+        env:
+          NVIM_APPNAME: nvim-mini
+        run: nvim --headless +qa
+
+      - name: Custom health check
+        env:
+          NVIM_APPNAME: nvim-mini
+        run: nvim --headless "+checkhealth nvim_mini" +qa
+
+      - name: Parley review test
+        run: nvim --headless -l tests/parley_review_spec.lua

@@ -0,0 +1,42 @@
+name: CI
+
+on:
+  push:
+  pull_request:
+
+jobs:
+  validate:
+    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
+
+    steps:
+      - name: Checkout repository
+        uses: actions/checkout@v4
+
+      - name: Install Neovim
+        run: |
+          curl -fsSL -o nvim.tar.gz https://github.com/neovim/neovim/releases/download/stable/nvim-linux-x86_64.tar.gz
+          tar -xzf nvim.tar.gz
+          echo "$PWD/nvim-linux-x86_64/bin" >> "$GITHUB_PATH"
+
+      - name: Install config into NVIM_APPNAME
+        run: |
+          mkdir -p "$HOME/.config/nvim-mini"
+          cp -R . "$HOME/.config/nvim-mini"
+
+      - name: Sync plugins
+        env:
+          NVIM_APPNAME: nvim-mini
+        run: nvim --headless "+Lazy! sync" +qa
+
+      - name: Headless startup check
+        env:
+          NVIM_APPNAME: nvim-mini
+        run: nvim --headless +qa
+
+      - name: Custom health check
+        env:
+          NVIM_APPNAME: nvim-mini
+        run: nvim --headless "+checkhealth nvim_mini" +qa
+
+      - name: Parley review test
+        run: nvim --headless -l tests/parley_review_spec.lua

# Skill: neovim

# Neovim Config — Modify, Add, Debug

Guided workflow for safely modifying this neovim configuration. Routes to the correct file, follows conventions, and validates changes.

## Arguments

- `$ARGUMENTS` — Natural language description of what to do. Examples:
  - `add plugin neo-tree` → Phase 1 (Add Plugin)
  - `add keymap for format on save` → Phase 2 (Add Keymap)
  - `configure gopls` → Phase 3 (LSP)
  - `fix colorscheme bleeding` → Phase 4 (Debug)
  - No args → show this help and ask what to do

## Phase 1: Add Plugin

### Step 1 — Check for conflicts

```bash
# Check if plugin or similar functionality already exists
grep -r "PLUGIN_NAME_OR_KEYWORD" lua/plugins/ --include="*.lua" -l
# Check lazy-lock for existing installation
grep "PLUGIN_NAME" lazy-lock.json
```

If the plugin or equivalent functionality already exists, report it and ask whether to replace or extend.

### Step 2 — Create the plugin spec

Create `lua/plugins/<plugin-name>.lua` with this template:

```lua
-- <Plugin Description>
-- <Why this plugin exists / what it replaces>

return {
  {
    "<owner>/<repo>",
    -- Lazy-loading (pick ONE appropriate trigger):
    -- event = "VeryLazy",           -- load after UI
    -- event = "BufReadPost",        -- load when opening files
    -- ft = { "go", "rust" },        -- load for specific filetypes
    -- cmd = { "CommandName" },      -- load on command
    -- keys = { ... },               -- load on keypress
    dependencies = {
      -- Only list DIRECT dependencies
    },
    opts = {
      -- ...

state.title: agentic|codecompanion|claude-code

content.summary:
(b2)
Continuation after user switched to build mode. Work proceeded in `~/neovim` to remove all AI plugins except `opencode.nvim` and new `sidekick.nvim`.

Validated Sidekick API against `https://raw.githubusercontent.com/folke/sidekick.nvim/main/README.md`. Confirmed `folke/sidekick.nvim` requires Neovim >= 0.11.2, uses `require("sidekick").setup`, provides NES via Copilot LSP, exposes CLI APIs `require("sidekick.cli").toggle/select/prompt/send`, and NES APIs `require("sidekick").nes_jump_or_apply()`, `require("sidekick.nes").update()`, and `require("sidekick.nes").clear()`. Confirmed Copilot NES requires `copilot-language-server` enabled through `vim.lsp.enable`; CLI integration works without Copilot. Current machine has Neovim 0.12.1 and `copilot-language-server` is absent, so the config must skip Copilot LSP when missing.

Edits already applied:
- Deleted `~/neovim/lua/plugins/codecompanion.lua`, `~/neovim/lua/plugins/claude-code.lua`, and `~/neovim/lua/plugins/agentic.lua`.
- Added `~/neovim/lua/plugins/sidekick.lua` with `folke/sidekick.nvim`. It defines helper `cli_send(msg)` that calls `require("sidekick.cli").send({ msg = msg })`. Keymaps: `<leader>asc` toggles OpenCode CLI via `require("sidekick.cli").toggle({ name = "opencode", focus = true })`; `<leader>ass` selects a Sidekick CLI; `<leader>asp` opens Sidekick prompt; `<leader>ast` sends `{this}`; `<leader>asf` sends `{file}`; `<leader>asv` sends `{selection}`; `<leader>asj` calls `require("sidekick").nes_jump_or_apply()`; `<leader>asu` calls `require("sidekick.nes").update()`; `<leader>asx` calls `require("sidekick.nes").clear()`. Options set `cli.watch = true`, tools `opencode`, `claude`, `codex`, `gemini`, and learning-oriented prompts `explain`, `diagnostics`, `review`, `tests`.
- Updated `~/neovim/lua/plugins/which-key.lua` by removing stale groups `<leader>aa` agentic, `<leader>aC` claude-code, and `<leader>ac` codecompanion, and adding `<leader>as` group `sidekick`; `<leader>ao` remains OpenCode.
- Updated `~/neovim/lua/plugins/lualine.lua` by removing Agentic filetypes from disabled `statusline` and `winbar`; now disabled statusline filetypes are only `{ "dashboard", "alpha", "starter", "snacks_dashboard" }` and `winbar = {}`.
- Updated `~/neovim/lua/plugins/blink-cmp.lua` by removing CodeCompanion from `sources.default`, deleting the `codecompanion` provider block, and later removing stale `AgenticInput` from the completion `enabled` guard. The guard is now `return vim.bo.filetype ~= "gwtt-prompt" and vim.bo.buftype ~= "prompt"`.
- Updated `~/neovim/lua/plugins/render-markdown.lua` by removing stale `AgenticChat` from `ft` and `opts.file_types`; both are now only `{ "markdown" }`.
- Updated `~/neovim/CLAUDE.md` AI Workflow to say: prefer one delivery harness per task; use `opencode.nvim` as the Neovim bridge into OpenCode and `sidekick.nvim` for learning-oriented Copilot NES / CLI assist; treat Neovim as a context router into OpenCode and hooks, not a separate orchestration layer.
- Updated `~/neovim/AGENTS.md` workflow contract similarly: use `opencode.nvim` as the Neovim bridge into OpenCode and `sidekick.nvim` for learning-oriented Copilot NES / CLI assist.
- Updated `~/neovim/.claude/skills/neovim/SKILL.md` leader-group table: `<leader>a` now says `AI / OpenCode / Sidekick` with examples `<leader>ao` OpenCode, `<leader>as` Sidekick instead of CodeCompanion.

Copilot LSP wiring was added to `~/neovim/lua/plugins/lsp.lua`:
- Added helper functions `copilot_sign_in(bufnr, client)` and `copilot_sign_out(_, client)` near existing helper functions. These mirror upstream nvim-lspconfig Copilot behavior, using `client:request("signIn", vim.empty_dict(), ...)` / `client:request("signOut", vim.empty_dict(), ...)`, copying `result.userCode` to `+` and `*`, prompting to open browser, calling `client:exec_cmd(result.command, { bufnr = bufnr }, ...)`, and notifying for `PromptUserDeviceFlow`, `AlreadySignedIn`, and `NotSignedIn` statuses.
- Added `copilot` entry to the existing `servers` table near GraphQL / buf LSPs. Config: `cmd = get_lsp_cmd("copilot-language-server", "--stdio")`, `root_markers = { ".git" }`, `init_options.editorInfo = { name = "Neovim", version = tostring(vim.version()) }`, `init_options.editorPluginInfo = { name = "sidekick.nvim", version = "1.0.0" }`, `settings.telemetry.telemetryLevel = "all"`, and `on_attach` creates buffer commands `:LspCopilotSignIn` and `:LspCopilotSignOut`. Because `cmd` uses `get_lsp_cmd`, this server is skipped when `copilot-language-server` is not installed.

Plugin lock handling:
- Ran `nvim --headless '+Lazy! sync sidekick.nvim' +qa` in `/Users/shahe.islam/neovim`. It successfully cloned `sidekick.nvim` to `/Users/shahe.islam/.local/share/nvim/lazy/sidekick.nvim`, cleaned the removed `agentic.nvim`, `claude-code.nvim`, and `codecompanion.nvim` plugin installs, and added Sidekick to the lockfile. It also unintentionally refreshed several unrelated plugin pins.
- To avoid unrelated lock churn, manually restored all unrelated `lazy-lock.json` commits via `apply_patch`. Current intended `lazy-lock.json` diff is narrowed to: removal of `agentic.nvim`, `claude-code.nvim`, and `codecompanion.nvim` entries; addition of `sidekick.nvim` at commit `208e1c5b8170c01fd1d07df0139322a76479b235`. Verified with `git diff -- lazy-lock.json`.

Search/cleanup state:
- Broad grep over entire repo produced many irrelevant hits under `.git` and `.entire`; ignore those runtime/history matches.
- Relevant targeted greps after cleanup found no stale `agentic`, `codecompanion`, `CodeCompanion`, `Agentic`, `claude-code.nvim`, or `ClaudeCode` references in `~/neovim/lua/**/*.lua`, `~/neovim/.claude/**/*.md`, or `lazy-lock.json`.
- `lua/config/hotreload.lua` still contains a comment/link mentioning Claude Code integration/Anthropic article. This is not a removed plugin spec reference and was intentionally left alone.

Current `git status --short` in `/Users/shahe.islam/neovim` shows intended changes only: modified `.claude/skills/neovim/SKILL.md`, `AGENTS.md`, `CLAUDE.md`, `lazy-lock.json`, `lua/plugins/blink-cmp.lua`, `lua/plugins/lsp.lua`, `lua/plugins/lualine.lua`, `lua/plugins/render-markdown.lua`, `lua/plugins/which-key.lua`; deleted `lua/plugins/agentic.lua`, `lua/plugins/claude-code.lua`, `lua/plugins/codecompanion.lua`; untracked `lua/plugins/sidekick.lua`.

Next steps: run formatting/syntax validation for Lua files and Neovim headless startup/checkhealth. If failures occur, fix them. Then report summary and note that `copilot-language-server` is still not installed, so Sidekick CLI works but NES requires installing/signing in to Copilot.

parts.text:
**Finalizing change summary**

I need to be concise but mention that the untracked file, sidekick.lua, isn't included in the diff --stat by default. It's important to ensure I note sidekick's addition, even if it's untracked, so the final status indicates it's not committed. Instead of saying "Done," I’ll phrase it as "Implemented in /neovim." I'll keep the summary formatted with single-level bullet points and highlight that lazy-lock now includes sidekick while removing others. Also, I'll note that `Lazy! check` succeeded with no extra lock modifications.

**Summarizing key points**

I need to mention the tests conducted, like Lua syntax parsing, Sidekick headless loading, and full Neovim headless startup, along with a git diff --check. Also, the `copilot-language-server` is absent, and while the Sidekick CLI works, the NES won't function until it's installed and signed in. I could suggest installing it with `bun add -g copilot-language-server`, but I should be cautious about uncertain details. I'll note that the LSP config is guarded to skip until it's installed, especially since the user requested changes.

Implemented in `~/neovim`.

Changed:
- Removed AI plugins: `agentic.nvim`, `claude-code.nvim`, `codecompanion.nvim`.
- Added `folke/sidekick.nvim` in `lua/plugins/sidekick.lua`.
- Kept `opencode.nvim` as the canonical OpenCode bridge.
- Cleaned stale AI references from `which-key`, `lualine`, `blink-cmp`, `render-markdown`, docs, and Neovim skill docs.
- Added guarded Copilot LSP wiring for Sidekick NES in `lua/plugins/lsp.lua`.
- Updated `lazy-lock.json` to remove the deleted plugins and add `sidekick.nvim`.

Validated:
- `nvim --headless -u NONE ... loadfile(...)` passed for edited Lua files.
- `nvim --headless '+Lazy! load sidekick.nvim' +qa` passed.
- `nvim --headless +qa` passed.
- `git diff --check` passed.
- Lockfile diff is limited to the intended AI plugin changes.

Note: `copilot-language-server` is not currently installed, so Sidekick’s CLI integration works now, but NES will stay inactive until that binary is installed and Copilot is signed in.

Does the lazy-lock.json need to be in gitignore? Its constantly being updated, does it matter?

No, `lazy-lock.json` generally should stay committed for a personal Neovim config.

It matters because it pins exact plugin commits. Without it, a fresh install or another machine can pull newer plugin versions and break your config unexpectedly.

Why it keeps changing:
- `:Lazy update` changes it intentionally.
- `:Lazy sync` can also update installed plugins depending on state/config.
- `:Lazy check` may fetch metadata but should not normally rewrite the lockfile.
- If plugins are unpinned or you run update-like commands, Lazy will refresh commits.

Recommended policy:
- Keep `lazy-lock.json` in git.
- Commit it only when you intentionally update plugins or add/remove plugins.
- If it changes accidentally after a check/sync, inspect with `git diff lazy-lock.json`; if it is unrelated churn, discard it.
- Avoid committing vague `lock` commits unless you intentionally want those plugin updates.

Your recent `lock` commit means 9 plugin pins changed. That may be fine if you intended a plugin update, but it is separate from the Sidekick cleanup.
If I get a free CoPilot subscription can we test sidekick.nvim?

**Testing LSP Setup**

I'm trying to figure out the README’s instructions, but I'm not so sure about them. I need to focus on the lazy loading the plugin with keymaps. First, I’ll verify the binary by running `command -v copilot-language-server`. Then I'll open nvim in the git repository. After that, I plan to check if copilot is attached by using `:LspInfo` or `:checkhealth vim.lsp`. Let’s see if this all works as intended!

Yes. If Copilot Free enables access for your GitHub account, we can test `sidekick.nvim`.

What we’ll need:
- `copilot-language-server` installed on PATH.
- GitHub Copilot enabled on your account.
- Sign in from Neovim with `:LspCopilotSignIn`.
- Open a real project file in a git repo so the Copilot LSP attaches.

What we can test:
- Sidekick CLI panel with OpenCode: `<leader>asc`.
- Sidekick prompt/select/send flows: `<leader>asp`, `<leader>ass`, `<leader>ast`, `<leader>asf`, `<leader>asv`.
- NES behavior after editing code: `<leader>asu` to request/update, `<leader>asj` to jump/apply, `<leader>asx` to clear.

Caveat: Sidekick’s CLI features do not require Copilot. Only NES requires Copilot plus `copilot-language-server`. If Copilot Free does not expose NES for your account, the plugin may load fine but NES won’t produce suggestions.
I have CoPilot free how can we test it
I’ll check only readiness signals in read-only mode: whether the Copilot language server is on PATH and whether the Neovim config tree is clean enough to test.

4. Open a real code file inside a git repo.

5. Run Copilot sign-in:
   `:LspCopilotSignIn`

6. Check attachment:
   `:LspInfo`
   You should see `copilot` attached if the binary is installed and the file is in a git-backed project.

7. Test Sidekick CLI, which does not require Copilot:
   `<leader>asc` opens Sidekick’s OpenCode CLI panel.

8. Test Sidekick NES, which does require Copilot:
   `<leader>asu` requests/updates NES.
   `<leader>asj` jumps to or applies the suggestion.
   `<leader>asx` clears it.

Because plan mode is active, I did not install anything. If you want me to run the setup/test flow, switch to build mode and I’ll do everything except the browser-side GitHub authorization, which you’ll need to approve.

parts.text:
**Finalizing bun command recommendations**

I need to clarify the final command for the bun package manager, especially regarding registry overrides. If the user faced a 401 error, adding with `bun add -g` might work for global installations. If the shell environment overrides are set, I could recommend using `env npm_config_registry=https://registry.npmjs.org/ bun add -g ...`. Also, bun supports the `--registry` flag. It's important to avoid editing configs if the user needs a temporary solution, and they might want to check with `bun pm config`.

**Clarifying package installation options**

I need to address whether the 401 error is due to an expired CodeArtifact auth or if the package isn't mirrored. There are two options to consider: using a public registry as a one-off is the recommended approach, while refreshing the CodeArtifact token would be necessary for continued access to the work registry. I should focus on presenting the final commands concisely without any nested bullets—for clarity and ease of use for the user.

The package exists on public npm. Your failure is because Bun is pointed at AWS CodeArtifact:

`registry-prod-904690835784.d.codeartifact.eu-west-2.amazonaws.com`

Use the public registry just for this install:

```bash
bun add -g @github/copilot-language-server --registry=https://registry.npmjs.org/
```

Then verify:

```bash
command -v copilot-language-server
copilot-language-server --version
```

If that still hits CodeArtifact, force the env for one command:

```bash
env npm_config_registry=https://registry.npmjs.org/ bun add -g @github/copilot-language-server
```

After that, test in Neovim:

```vim
:LspCopilotSignIn
:LspInfo
```

Then test Sidekick NES with:
- `<leader>asu` update/request NES
- `<leader>asj` jump/apply NES
- `<leader>asx` clear NES

I would not add `lazy-lock.json` or npm registry config changes for this. This is just a one-off public registry bypass.

Why did it say win32?

❯ bun add -g @github/copilot-language-server --registry=https://registry.npmjs.org/
bun add v1.3.12 (700fc117)
  🔍 @github/copilot-language-server-win32-x64 [26/28]

That is normal for this package.

`@github/copilot-language-server` publishes platform-specific optional packages, including names like:

- `@github/copilot-language-server-darwin-arm64`
- `@github/copilot-language-server-darwin-x64`
- `@github/copilot-language-server-linux-x64`
- `@github/copilot-language-server-win32-x64`

Bun showing `@github/copilot-language-server-win32-x64 [26/28]` means it is resolving/checking one of the optional platform packages in the dependency set. It does not necessarily mean it will install or run the Windows binary.

After install, verify the actual binary:

```bash
command -v copilot-language-server
copilot-language-server --version
file "$(command -v copilot-language-server)"
```

On your Mac, `file` should indicate a macOS script/binary path, not a Windows executable.
But i'm running on MacOS?

Yes, you’re on macOS. The `win32` line is just Bun processing optional platform packages published by the Copilot LSP package.

Many npm packages ship like this:

- Main package: `@github/copilot-language-server`
- Optional platform packages: `darwin-arm64`, `darwin-x64`, `linux-*`, `win32-*`

Package managers often enumerate all optional dependencies during resolution. They then skip or ignore the ones that don’t match your OS/CPU. So seeing:

```text
@g‌ithub/copilot-language-server-win32-x64 [26/28]
```

does not mean Bun thinks your Mac is Windows. It means dependency resolution reached optional package 26 of 28.

The important check is what got linked:

```bash
command -v copilot-language-server
file "$(command -v copilot-language-server)"
copilot-language-server --version
```

If the install succeeded on macOS, `command -v` should point to Bun’s global bin, and the executable should launch normally.

❯ command -v copilot-language-server
  file "$(command -v copilot-language-server)"
  copilot-language-server --version
/Users/shahe.islam/.bun/bin/copilot-language-server
/Users/shahe.islam/.bun/bin/copilot-language-server: symbolic link to ../install/global/node_modules/@github/copilot-language-server/dist/language-server.js
1.480.0

That confirms the install is correct on macOS.

`file` reports a symlink to:

```text
../install/global/node_modules/@github/copilot-language-server/dist/language-server.js
```

and the server runs:

```text
1.480.0
```

Next test steps in Neovim:

1. Restart Neovim so PATH/LSP config picks up `copilot-language-server`.

2. Open a real code file inside a git repo.

3. Run:

```vim
:LspCopilotSignIn
```

4. Complete the GitHub browser authorization.

5. Confirm attachment:

```vim
:LspInfo
```

You should see `copilot` attached to the buffer.

6. Test Sidekick NES:

```text
<leader>asu  request/update NES
<leader>asj  jump/apply NES
<leader>asx  clear NES
```

7. Test Sidekick CLI separately:

```text
<leader>asc  open Sidekick OpenCode CLI panel
```

If `:LspCopilotSignIn` does not exist, then the Copilot LSP did not attach. The most likely causes are: Neovim wasn’t restarted, the opened file is not inside a git repo, or `copilot-language-server` is not visible in Neovim’s PATH.

parts.text:
**Considering notifications improvement**

I’m thinking of how to enhance the build mode by changing `LspCopilotSignIn` to use `vim.api.nvim_echo` or `vim.notify(..., timeout=false/30000)`. I might also add a keymap like `<leader>mn` for notification history, which could be handy. Another option is routing `vim.notify` back through Noice by removing the override and enabling the Noice notification. However, in plan mode, I should avoid any edits and keep things concise.

Because that Copilot device-code message is a `vim.notify()` notification, not a normal `:messages` / Noice message.

In your config:
- `:LspCopilotSignIn` calls `vim.notify("Enter your one-time code ...")` from `lua/plugins/lsp.lua`.
- `lua/plugins/noice.lua` overrides `vim.notify` to send directly to `nvim-notify`.
- Noice notification capture is disabled with `notify.enabled = false`.
- So `<leader>mh` and `<leader>ml` show Noice history, but this message lives in `nvim-notify` history instead.

Try this while still in the same Neovim session:

```vim
:Notifications
```

That should show the notification history from `nvim-notify`.

Also, for this specific Copilot sign-in message, the config copies the one-time code to your clipboard before showing the notification, so you should be able to paste it into GitHub even after the popup disappears.

Current code from your screenshot:

```text
82CA-A8CC
```

If you want, once we leave plan mode I’d change the Copilot sign-in notification to be harder to lose, probably by making that specific message persistent/long-lived or by also printing it through Noice history.
So errors are via Noice and notifications are via :Notifications?

Mostly, yes, with one nuance.

In your current config:

- `vim.notify(...)` notifications go to `nvim-notify`, so view them with `:Notifications`.
- Noice message history via `<leader>mh` / `<leader>ml` covers Noice-managed messages like command output, `msg_show`, `:messages` style output, and routed UI messages.
- Errors can appear in either place depending on how they are emitted.

Examples:
- A plugin calls `vim.notify("error", vim.log.levels.ERROR)` -> `:Notifications`.
- Neovim emits an error/message through `msg_show` -> Noice history, so `<leader>mh` / `<leader>ml`.
- LSP progress is mostly skipped by your Noice routes.
- Your Copilot sign-in code is `vim.notify`, so it is in `:Notifications`, not `<leader>mh`.

So the mental model is:

```text
vim.notify(...)        -> :Notifications
Neovim message stream  -> <leader>mh / <leader>ml
```

If we want one place for both, we can change the Noice config later to capture `vim.notify` too, or add a keymap like `<leader>mn` for `:Notifications`.

The way <leader>ml and <leader>mh show the messages in a split buffer at the bottom, can we have the same type for <leader>n for Notifications? This would be better aligned, let me know if there are any conflicts with my suggestion

**Investigating keymap conflicts**

I'm currently in read-only mode, and I need to explore any conflicts that might be happening. Specifically, I should inspect keymaps to see if there are conflicts, especially with the <leader>n combination. I think a search will help me figure out if any mapping issues are arising. It's a bit of a puzzle, but I'm keen to resolve these conflicts so everything runs smoothly!
I’ll check existing `<leader>n` bindings and notification APIs so the plan accounts for real conflicts.

---@param opts notify.Options Notification options
---@return notify.AsyncRecord
function notify.async(message, level, opts)
  if not global_instance then
    notify.setup()
  end
  return global_instance.async(message, level, opts)
end

--- Get records of all previous notifications
---
--- You can use the `:Notifications` command to display a log of previous notifications
---@param opts? notify.HistoryOpts
---@return notify.Record[]
function notify.history(opts)
  if not global_instance then
    notify.setup()
  end
  return global_instance.history(opts)
end

parts.text:
Yes, we can do that.

Conflict check:
- No direct `<leader>n` mapping found in your Neovim config.
- Existing notification-related binding is only GitHub/Octo inbox: `<leader>gon`.
- `<leader>m` is currently overloaded for Markdown plus your Noice message keys, so `<leader>n` is actually cleaner for notifications.
- No obvious conflict with your current `which-key` groups.

Best implementation:
- Add `<leader>n` to `lua/plugins/noice.lua`, since that file already owns `nvim-notify` setup and `<leader>mh` / `<leader>ml`.
- Instead of using `:Notifications`, build a scratch split buffer from `require("notify").history()`.
- Open it as a bottom split, matching the Noice split behavior: bottom, about `20%` height, wrapped lines, `q` / `<Esc>` closes.
- Include timestamp, level, title, and message body.
- Add maybe `<leader>nC` later for clearing history, but I would not add that unless you want it.

Behavior after change:
- `<leader>mh` = Noice message history in bottom split.
- `<leader>ml` = last Noice message in bottom split.
- `<leader>n` = notification history in bottom split.

Important nuance:
- Notification history is session-local. It will show notifications from the current Neovim session, not after restarting Neovim.

I’m in plan mode, so I haven’t edited anything. Switch to build mode and I’ll add the `<leader>n` split-buffer notification history.
Implement this

**Implementing Neovim Configuration**

I'm looking to implement something in Neovim while considering the current working directory and dotfiles. The instructions say not to create outside dotfiles, but I might have some exceptions for Neovim. I need to edit the noice.lua file, use apply_patch, and inspect the current status. I’m also thinking of a single modest task to keep track of, maybe with a helper function in the config. We have to ensure everything works correctly, especially concerning key triggers and lazy loading.

**Formatting Notification History**

I’m thinking about adding a local function in the keymap function, maybe making it bigger inline, but that’s fine. I could also define a function called `open_notification_history()` at the top, since the file returns a table and can require notify when called. I plan to set up the buffer to display notifications in a split view, ensuring it has the right properties. 

For the notification history, I want to include messages with timestamps and handle the data types properly while incorporating safeguards. The final goal would be a neatly formatted output!

**Defining Function to Format Lines**

I'm considering how to create a local function called `as_lines` that takes a value. If the type is a table, I want to iterate through its entries and split them into lines using `vim.split`. I need to keep it simple and not overcomplicate things, so I’ll focus on making it minimal while still achieving the desired outcome. I guess I just need to ensure the function handles different data types appropriately without adding extra complexity!
I’m adding `<leader>n` in the existing Noice/notify config so notification history opens in the same bottom split style as your Noice message history.

input.patchText:
*** Begin Patch
*** Update File: /Users/shahe.islam/neovim/lua/plugins/noice.lua
@@
--- Noice.nvim configuration to ensure text fits in message window
+-- Noice.nvim configuration to ensure text fits in message window
+local function open_notification_history()
+  local ok, notify = pcall(require, "notify")
+  if not ok then
+    vim.notify("nvim-notify is not available", vim.log.levels.WARN)
+    return
+  end
+
+  local history = notify.history()
+  local lines = {}
+
+  if #history == 0 then
+    lines = { "No notifications in history." }
+  else
+    for _, notif in ipairs(history) do
+      local time = notif.time and vim.fn.strftime("%H:%M:%S", notif.time) or "--:--:--"
+      local level = notif.level and tostring(notif.level) or "INFO"
+      local title = ""
+
+      if type(notif.title) == "table" then
+        title = table.concat(notif.title, " ")
+      elseif notif.title then
+        title = tostring(notif.title)
+      end
+
+      local header = string.format("[%s] %s", time, level)
+      if title ~= "" then
+        header = header .. " " .. title
+      end
+      table.insert(lines, header)
+
+      if type(notif.message) == "table" then
+        for _, message_line in ipairs(notif.message) do
+          table.insert(lines, tostring(message_line))
+        end
+      elseif notif.message then
+        for _, message_line in ipairs(vim.split(tostring(notif.message), "\n", { plain = true })) do
+          table.insert(lines, message_line)
+        end
+      end
+
+      table.insert(lines, "")
+    end
+  end
+
+  vim.cmd("botright split")
+  vim.cmd("resize " .. math.max(1, math.floor(vim.o.lines * 0.2)))
+
+  local buf = vim.api.nvim_create_buf(false, true)
+  local win = vim.api.nvim_get_current_win()
+  vim.api.nvim_win_set_buf(win, buf)
+  vim.api.nvim_buf_set_name(buf, "Notifications")
+  vim.api.nvim_buf_set_lines(buf, 0, -1, false, lines)
+  vim.bo[buf].buftype = "nofile"
+  vim.bo[buf].bufhidden = "wipe"
+  vim.bo[buf].swapfile = false
+  vim.bo[buf].filetype = "notify-history"
+  vim.bo[buf].modifiable = false
+  vim.wo[win].wrap = true
+  vim.wo[win].linebreak = true
+  vim.wo[win].cursorline = false
+
+  local close = function()
+    if vim.api.nvim_win_is_valid(win) then
+      vim.api.nvim_win_close(win, true)
+    end
+  end
+  vim.keymap.set("n", "q", close, { buffer = buf, silent = true, desc = "Close Notifications" })
+  vim.keymap.set("n", "<Esc>", close, { buffer = buf, silent = true, desc = "Close Notifications" })
+
+  vim.api.nvim_win_set_cursor(win, { vim.api.nvim_buf_line_count(buf), 0 })
+end
+
 return {
@@
     -- Quick access to history (in addition to LazyVim's <leader>snh)
     { "<leader>mh", function() require("noice").cmd("history") end, desc = "Message History" },
     { "<leader>ml", function() vim.cmd("Noice last split") end, desc = "Last Message" },
     { "<leader>md", function() require("noice").cmd("dismiss") end, desc = "Dismiss Messages" },
+    { "<leader>n", open_notification_history, desc = "Notification History" },
     -- Toggle persistent messages
*** End Patch

parts.text:
Implemented `<leader>n` in `~/neovim/lua/plugins/noice.lua`.

What it does:
- Opens `nvim-notify` history in a bottom split.
- Uses about 20% window height, wrap, and linebreak.
- Shows timestamp, level, title, and message body.
- `q` or `<Esc>` closes the notification split.
- No direct `<leader>n` conflict found.

Validated:
- `nvim --headless -u NONE ... loadfile('lua/plugins/noice.lua')` passed.
- `nvim --headless +qa` passed.

Working tree now has one modified file: `lua/plugins/noice.lua`.
Okay now give me a specific example on how to test sidekick.nvim

Concrete test using this Neovim repo:

1. Open a Lua file:

```bash
nvim lua/plugins/noice.lua
```

2. Confirm Copilot attached:

```vim
:LspInfo
```

Look for `copilot` in the active clients for the buffer.

3. If not signed in yet:

```vim
:LspCopilotSignIn
```

Open the GitHub device URL, enter the code, then return to Neovim.

4. Create a clear edit opportunity. Add this temporary function near the top, then pause:

```lua
local function sidekick_test_format_name(first, last)
  -- return the full name in "First Last" format
end
```

5. Request Sidekick NES:

```text
<leader>asu
```

6. Jump to or apply the suggestion:

```text
<leader>asj
```

Expected result: Sidekick/Copilot should suggest something like:

```lua
return first .. " " .. last
```

7. Clear the suggestion if needed:

```text
<leader>asx
```

8. Test Sidekick CLI separately:

```text
<leader>asc
```

Expected result: Sidekick opens its OpenCode CLI panel. Then try:

```text
<leader>asf
```

Expected result: it sends the current file context to the Sidekick CLI.

Do not keep the temporary `sidekick_test_format_name` function afterward unless you want it committed.

parts.text:
Yes. In this config, use:

```text
<leader>ls
```

That calls your custom `_G.lsp_status()` and shows active LSP clients via notification history.

For a direct command alternative, run:

```vim
:lua vim.print(vim.lsp.get_clients({ bufnr = 0 }))
```

In your screenshot, `LspCopilotSignIn` existing is already a strong signal that the `copilot` LSP attached, because that command is created in the Copilot `on_attach`.

So for Sidekick testing, the quick checks are:

```vim
:LspCopilotSignIn
```

Then:

```text
<leader>ls
<leader>n
```

`<leader>ls` shows active LSPs as a notification, and `<leader>n` opens notification history in the bottom split we just added.

`LspInfo` likely isn’t available because newer Neovim/LSP setups don’t always expose the old `nvim-lspconfig` `:LspInfo` command. Your config’s intended replacement is `<leader>ls`.

```vim
